news_summary: Recent breakthroughs show promising treatments reversing deafness, improving Crohn’s symptoms rapidly through diet, and developing a versatile anti-viral pill, while new genetic insights advance understanding of schizophrenia.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes ouvrent la voie à des traitements prometteurs permettant de remédier à la surdité, d'atténuer rapidement les symptômes de la maladie de Crohn grâce à un régime alimentaire et de mettre au point un médicament antiviral polyvalent, tandis que de nouvelles découvertes en génétique permettent de mieux comprendre la schizophrénie.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan tratamientos prometedores para revertir la sordera, mejorar rápidamente los síntomas de la enfermedad de Crohn mediante la dieta y desarrollar un medicamento antiviral versátil, mientras que los nuevos descubrimientos genéticos permiten comprender mejor la esquizofrenia.

articles.title: Deafness reversed: One injection restores hearing in just weeks
articles.summary: A new gene therapy is giving people born deaf the chance to hear, often within just weeks. In a small but groundbreaking study, researchers delivered a working copy of a key hearing gene directly into the inner ear using a single injection. All ten patients, ranging from young children to adults, experienced improved hearing, with some showing rapid gains in just one month.
articles.content:
A new study shows that gene therapy can significantly improve hearing in people born with congenital deafness or severe hearing loss. Researchers at Karolinska Institutet, working with hospitals and universities in China, treated ten patients and saw hearing improve in every case. The therapy was also well-tolerated. The findings were published in the journal Nature Medicine.

"This is a huge step forward in the genetic treatment of deafness, one that can be life-changing for children and adults," says Maoli Duan, consultant and docent at the Department of Clinical Science, Intervention and Technology, Karolinska Institutet, Sweden, and one of the study's corresponding authors.

Targeting the OTOF Gene

The trial included ten patients between the ages of 1 and 24 who were treated at five hospitals in China. All had a genetic form of deafness linked to mutations in a gene called OTOF. These mutations prevent the body from producing enough of the protein otoferlin, which is essential for sending sound signals from the inner ear to the brain.

Rapid Results After a Single Injection

To address this, researchers used a synthetic adeno-associated virus (AAV) to deliver a working version of the OTOF gene directly into the inner ear. The treatment was given as a single injection through a membrane at the base of the cochlea known as the round window.

The effects appeared quickly. Most patients began to regain some hearing within one month. After six months, all participants showed clear improvement. On average, the level of sound they could detect improved from 106 decibels to 52.

Strongest Gains Seen in Younger Patients

Children showed the most dramatic responses, especially those between the ages of five and eight. One seven-year-old girl regained nearly full hearing and was able to have everyday conversations with her mother just four months after treatment. At the same time, the therapy also produced meaningful improvements in adult patients.

"Smaller studies in China have previously shown positive results in children, but this is the first time that the method has been tested in teenagers and adults, too," says Dr. Duan. "Hearing was greatly improved in many of the participants, which can have a profound effect on their life quality. We will now be following these patients to see how lasting the effect is."

Treatment Found To Be Safe

The therapy was shown to be safe and well-tolerated. The most commonly reported side effect was a decrease in neutrophils, which are a type of white blood cell. No serious adverse reactions were observed during the follow-up period of 6 to 12 months.

Expanding Gene Therapy for Hearing Loss

"OTOF is just the beginning," says Dr. Duan. "We and other researchers are expanding our work to other, more common genes that cause deafness, such as GJB2 and TMC1. These are more complicated to treat, but animal studies have so far returned promising results. We are confident that patients with different kinds of genetic deafness will one day be able to receive treatment."

The research involved multiple institutions, including Zhongda Hospital at Southeast University in China. Funding came from several Chinese research programs as well as Otovia Therapeutics Inc., the company that developed the gene therapy and employs many of the researchers involved. A full list of disclosures and conflicts of interest is available in the published paper.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough in gene therapy that restores hearing in people born deaf, demonstrating rapid and meaningful improvements in patients of various ages. The therapy addresses a genetic cause of deafness, has broad potential impact for many affected individuals worldwide, and is supported by detailed clinical trial results and expert commentary.
articles.personality_title: New gene therapy restores hearing in deaf patients after one injection
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some people are born deaf because of changes in a gene called OTOF. This gene helps send sound signals from the ear to the brain. Without it working properly, people cannot hear well or at all.

**What happened** – Scientists at Karolinska Institutet and hospitals in China gave ten deaf patients, aged 1 to 24, a single injection of a new gene therapy. This therapy used a harmless virus to deliver a working copy of the OTOF gene directly into the inner ear. Within a month, many patients began to hear better, and after six months, all showed clear improvement.

**Impact** – This is the first time this gene therapy has been tested successfully in both children and adults. The treatment helped patients hear sounds much more clearly — on average, their hearing improved from very loud sounds (106 decibels) to much quieter ones (52 decibels). A seven-year-old girl regained almost full hearing and could talk with her mother just four months after treatment. The therapy was also safe, with only mild side effects reported.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to watch these patients over time to see how long the hearing improvements last. They are also working on similar treatments for other genes that cause deafness. These new therapies could help even more people in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single gene therapy injection has safely restored hearing in deaf patients, showing promise for treating genetic deafness in both children and adults.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle thérapie génique restaure l’audition après une seule injection
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certaines personnes naissent sourdes à cause d’un changement dans un gène appelé OTOF. Ce gène aide à transmettre les sons de l’oreille au cerveau. Sans ce gène qui fonctionne bien, ces personnes n’entendent pas ou très mal.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du Karolinska Institutet et d’hôpitaux en Chine ont donné à dix patients sourds, âgés de 1 à 24 ans, une injection unique d’une nouvelle thérapie génique. Cette thérapie utilise un virus inoffensif pour envoyer une copie fonctionnelle du gène OTOF directement dans l’oreille interne. En un mois, plusieurs patients ont commencé à mieux entendre, et après six mois, tous ont montré une amélioration nette.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que cette thérapie génique est testée avec succès chez des enfants et des adultes. Le traitement a permis aux patients d’entendre des sons beaucoup plus faibles — en moyenne, leur seuil d’audition est passé de 106 décibels à 52 décibels. Une fillette de sept ans a presque retrouvé une audition complète et pouvait parler avec sa mère seulement quatre mois après le traitement. La thérapie était aussi sûre, avec seulement des effets secondaires légers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont suivre ces patients pour voir combien de temps dure l’amélioration de l’audition. Ils travaillent aussi sur des traitements similaires pour d’autres gènes responsables de la surdité. Ces nouvelles thérapies pourraient aider encore plus de personnes à l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une injection unique de thérapie génique a restauré l’audition chez des patients sourds, montrant un espoir pour traiter la surdité génétique chez les enfants et les adultes.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva terapia génica restaura la audición tras una sola inyección
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunas personas nacen sordas debido a un cambio en un gen llamado OTOF. Este gen ayuda a enviar señales de sonido del oído al cerebro. Si no funciona bien, no pueden oír.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Instituto Karolinska y hospitales en China dieron a diez pacientes sordos, de 1 a 24 años, una sola inyección de una nueva terapia génica. Esta terapia usó un virus seguro para llevar una copia funcional del gen OTOF directamente al oído interno. En un mes, muchos pacientes empezaron a oír mejor, y después de seis meses, todos mostraron una mejora clara.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que esta terapia se prueba con éxito en niños y adultos. El tratamiento ayudó a los pacientes a escuchar sonidos mucho más suaves — en promedio, mejoraron de escuchar sonidos muy fuertes (106 decibelios) a sonidos mucho más bajos (52 decibelios). Una niña de siete años recuperó casi toda su audición y pudo hablar con su mamá solo cuatro meses después del tratamiento. La terapia también fue segura, con efectos secundarios leves.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán a estos pacientes para ver cuánto duran las mejoras en la audición. También están trabajando en tratamientos similares para otros genes que causan sordera. Estas nuevas terapias podrían ayudar a más personas en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una sola inyección de terapia génica restauró la audición en pacientes sordos, mostrando un avance para tratar la sordera genética en niños y adultos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized inner ear shaped like a delicate shell, with a glowing, gently swirling strand of DNA woven into its curves, surrounded by soft, flowing waves of sound represented as translucent, colorful ribbons moving from the ear toward a bright, abstract brain silhouette in the background, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: This 5-day diet helped Crohn’s patients feel better fast
articles.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that what people eat could finally offer real relief for Crohn’s disease, a condition that has long lacked clear dietary guidance. Researchers found that a “fasting-mimicking diet” — involving just five days a month of very low-calorie, plant-based meals — led to noticeable improvements in symptoms for most participants. Even more striking, the diet didn’t just make patients feel better; it also reduced key biological markers of inflammation linked to the disease.
articles.content:
"What should I eat?" is one of the most common questions people with inflammatory bowel disease ask their doctors.

It is also one of the hardest to answer. Inflammatory bowel disease, which includes ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, has not been studied extensively when it comes to diet. Large, well-controlled studies on dietary changes have been limited.

Now, researchers from Stanford Medicine and collaborating institutions may be closing that gap. In a national randomized controlled trial, they found that a short-term, calorie-restricted eating plan led to meaningful improvements in both symptoms and biological markers in people with mild-to-moderate Crohn's disease. The results were recently published in Nature Medicine.

Studying diet is challenging. Participants may not always accurately report what they eat, and placebo effects are difficult to avoid since people know which diet they are following. Even so, the results stood out. Patients not only reported feeling better, but also showed measurable reductions in inflammation in biological samples. The findings could help doctors give clearer dietary guidance to patients.

"We have been very limited in what kind of dietary information we can provide patients," said Sidhartha R. Sinha, MD, an assistant professor of gastroenterology and hepatology and the senior author on the paper. "This study will give physicians evidence to support recommendations in an area that patients are very curious about."

Crohn's Disease and Limited Treatment Options

Crohn's disease is a long-term condition that affects roughly one million Americans. It causes inflammation in the digestive tract and can lead to symptoms such as diarrhea, cramping, abdominal pain and weight loss.

For mild cases, steroids are currently the only approved treatment. However, they can cause serious side effects, especially when used over long periods.

How the Fasting Mimicking Diet Was Tested

The clinical trial followed 97 patients with mild-to-moderate Crohn's disease across the United States. Of these, 65 participants followed a fasting mimicking diet, while 32 continued with their usual eating habits as a control group. The study lasted three months.

Those in the fasting mimicking group reduced their calorie intake for five consecutive days each month, consuming roughly 700 to 1,100 calories per day. During this period, they were given plant-based meals. For the rest of the month, they returned to their normal diet.

Significant Symptom Improvement

By the end of the study, about two-thirds of the participants following the fasting mimicking diet reported improvement in their symptoms.

"We were very pleasantly surprised that the majority of patients seemed to benefit from this diet," Sinha said. "We noticed that even after just one FMD cycle, there were clinical benefits."

In contrast, fewer than half of those in the control group experienced symptom improvement. Researchers noted that these changes were likely due to natural fluctuations in the disease and ongoing standard treatments, such as medications.

Some people in the fasting mimicking group reported fatigue and headaches, but no serious side effects were observed.

Measurable Reductions in Inflammation

The researchers also looked beyond symptoms to understand what was happening inside the body.

Sinha's interest in the fasting mimicking diet came from earlier research showing it could lower levels of C-reactive protein, a marker of inflammation, in people with elevated baseline levels. "The effects seen on inflammatory markers made this an appealing diet to study in Crohn's disease since many patients with this disease also have elevated inflammatory markers," he said.

To explore this further, the team collected and analyzed biological samples, including blood and stool, to track changes in inflammation.

"Our goal in collecting these and other biospecimens was to dig deeper into why there's this differential response," Sinha said. "Can we find mechanisms to explain the findings and signatures that might help predict patients who will respond to the diet?"

They found that levels of fecal calprotectin, a protein that signals inflammation in the gut, dropped significantly in the fasting mimicking group compared with the control group. Other inflammation-related molecules, including certain lipid mediators derived from fatty acids, were also reduced. In addition, immune cells in these participants produced fewer inflammatory signals.

Researchers are now investigating whether shifts in the gut microbiome could help explain these benefits.

What Comes Next for Diet and Crohn's Research

"There's still a lot more to be done to understand the biology behind how this and other diets work in patients with Crohn's disease," Sinha said.

The study's first authors are Stanford Medicine's Chiraag Kulkarni, MD, an instructor in gastroenterology and hepatology, and assistant clinical research coordinator Touran Fardeen. Researchers from the University of Southern California and the University of California, San Francisco, also contributed.

Author Valter Longo, PhD, has equity interest in L-Nutra, the company from which the fasting mimicking meals were purchased and has filed patents related to the diet.

The research was supported by The Leona M. and Harry B. Helmsley Charitable Trust, the National Institutes of Health (grants UM1TR004921, 2L30 DK126220, T32DK007056, K08DK134856 and NIDDK R01DK085025), the Plant Based Diet Initiative at Stanford University, the Kenneth Rainin Foundation, the Doris Duke Foundation Physician Scientist Fellowship Award, a CZ Biohub Physician Scientist Scholar Award, the Colleen and Robert D. Hass fund, and the Chan-Zuckerberg Biohub Investigator Program.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a well-controlled clinical trial demonstrating that a fasting-mimicking diet significantly improves symptoms and reduces inflammation in Crohn's disease patients, a condition with limited treatment options. The findings have broad significance for millions affected by the disease, offering a tangible, evidence-based dietary intervention with measurable biological benefits. The study is detailed and focused on a single impactful breakthrough in health.
articles.personality_title: Five-day fasting diet eases symptoms for Crohn’s patients
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Crohn’s disease is a long-term illness that causes inflammation in the digestive system. It affects about one million people in the US and can cause pain, diarrhea, and weight loss. Treatments are limited, especially for mild cases, where steroids are often used but can have serious side effects.

**What happened** – Researchers from Stanford Medicine and other institutions tested a special diet called the fasting mimicking diet (FMD) in a study with 97 Crohn’s patients. For five days each month, 65 patients ate very low-calorie, plant-based meals (about 700 to 1,100 calories per day), then returned to their normal diet for the rest of the month. The other 32 patients continued their usual diet. This study lasted three months.

**Impact** – About two-thirds of the patients on the fasting mimicking diet felt better and showed clear signs that inflammation in their bodies decreased. The study measured lower levels of inflammation markers in blood and stool samples, which means the diet helped reduce the disease’s effects inside the body, not just symptoms. This is important because it is one of the first strong studies showing how diet can help manage Crohn’s disease.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will continue to study how this diet changes the gut and the immune system. They want to understand why some patients respond better than others and if changes in gut bacteria play a role. This research could lead to new dietary advice and treatments for Crohn’s disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A five-day monthly fasting mimicking diet helped many Crohn’s patients feel better and lowered inflammation, offering a promising new way to manage the disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un régime de cinq jours soulage les symptômes des patients atteints de la maladie de Crohn
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie de Crohn est une maladie chronique qui provoque une inflammation du système digestif. Elle touche environ un million de personnes aux États-Unis et peut causer des douleurs, des diarrhées et une perte de poids. Les traitements sont limités, notamment pour les cas légers où les stéroïdes sont souvent utilisés mais peuvent entraîner de graves effets secondaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Stanford Medicine et d’autres institutions ont testé un régime spécial appelé régime mimant le jeûne (FMD) chez 97 patients atteints de la maladie de Crohn. Pendant cinq jours chaque mois, 65 patients ont consommé des repas à très faible teneur en calories, à base de plantes (environ 700 à 1 100 calories par jour), puis sont revenus à leur régime normal pour le reste du mois. Les 32 autres patients ont continué leur régime habituel. L’étude a duré trois mois.

**Impact** – Environ deux tiers des patients suivant le régime mimant le jeûne se sont sentis mieux et ont montré des signes clairs de réduction de l’inflammation dans leur corps. L’étude a mesuré une baisse des marqueurs d’inflammation dans le sang et les échantillons de selles, ce qui signifie que le régime a aidé à réduire les effets de la maladie à l’intérieur du corps, pas seulement les symptômes. C’est important car c’est l’une des premières études solides montrant comment l’alimentation peut aider à gérer la maladie de Crohn.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier comment ce régime modifie l’intestin et le système immunitaire. Ils veulent comprendre pourquoi certains patients répondent mieux que d’autres et si les changements dans les bactéries intestinales jouent un rôle. Ces recherches pourraient conduire à de nouveaux conseils alimentaires et traitements pour la maladie de Crohn.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un régime de cinq jours par mois mimant le jeûne a aidé de nombreux patients atteints de la maladie de Crohn à se sentir mieux et à réduire l’inflammation, offrant une nouvelle piste prometteuse pour gérer la maladie.
articles.personality_title_es: Una dieta de cinco días ayuda a aliviar los síntomas de pacientes con Crohn
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Crohn es una condición crónica que causa inflamación en el sistema digestivo. Afecta a alrededor de un millón de personas en Estados Unidos y puede causar dolor, diarrea y pérdida de peso. Los tratamientos son limitados, especialmente en casos leves, donde los esteroides se usan con frecuencia pero pueden tener efectos secundarios graves.

**Qué ocurrió** – Investigadores de Stanford Medicine y otras instituciones probaron una dieta especial llamada dieta que imita el ayuno (FMD) en un estudio con 97 pacientes con Crohn. Durante cinco días cada mes, 65 pacientes consumieron comidas basadas en plantas y muy bajas en calorías (aproximadamente 700 a 1,100 calorías por día), y luego volvieron a su dieta normal el resto del mes. Los otros 32 pacientes continuaron con su dieta habitual. El estudio duró tres meses.

**Impacto** – Cerca de dos tercios de los pacientes que siguieron la dieta que imita el ayuno se sintieron mejor y mostraron señales claras de que la inflamación en sus cuerpos disminuyó. El estudio midió niveles más bajos de marcadores de inflamación en muestras de sangre y heces, lo que significa que la dieta ayudó a reducir los efectos de la enfermedad dentro del cuerpo, no solo los síntomas. Esto es importante porque es uno de los primeros estudios sólidos que muestra cómo la dieta puede ayudar a controlar la enfermedad de Crohn.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando cómo esta dieta cambia el intestino y el sistema inmunológico. Quieren entender por qué algunos pacientes responden mejor que otros y si los cambios en las bacterias intestinales tienen un papel. Esta investigación podría llevar a nuevos consejos dietéticos y tratamientos para la enfermedad de Crohn.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una dieta de cinco días al mes que imita el ayuno ayudó a muchos pacientes con Crohn a sentirse mejor y redujo la inflamación, ofreciendo una nueva forma prometedora de manejar la enfermedad.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently glowing digestive tract shaped like a flowing river, surrounded by vibrant, healthy plant-based foods arranged like stepping stones along its path, with soft, natural earth tones conveying healing and calmness.

articles.title: Multipurpose anti-viral pill may treat colds, norovirus, flu and covid
articles.summary: AI predicted that a forgotten breast cancer drug could be repurposed to treat many respiratory and gastrointestinal viruses, and subsequent animal tests suggests it may be right
articles.content:
AI predicted that a forgotten breast cancer drug could be repurposed to treat many respiratory and gastrointestinal viruses, and subsequent animal tests suggests it may be right

Viral RNA relies on an enzyme to replicate, which offers up a target to protect against a range of pathogens Juan Gaertner/Science Photo Library/Alamy

A single drug has been found to inhibit a range of common viruses in lab studies, including coronaviruses, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), norovirus, and influenza and hepatitis viruses. It will be tested in a clinical trial next year, raising hopes that the pill could one day be taken at home to relieve unpleasant symptoms or even limit infections if there were another viral pandemic.

“As far as we can tell, this is the first drug that’s ever demonstrated activity across all these viral families,” says Daniel Haders, co-founder of Model Medicines, the California-based company leading its development. If it is approved, Haders envisages it being a pill that people could take if, for example, they have a flu-like illness but don’t know if it is influenza, covid-19, RSV or something else.

The drug was originally developed as a breast cancer treatment named ERA-923, but it was abandoned in the early 2000s after showing little benefit in clinical trials. Now, an AI drug-discovery platform developed by Haders and his colleagues has figured out that the forgotten medicine may inhibit a range of viruses via an unrelated mechanism.

The platform was tasked with finding a drug that could block a viral enzyme called RNA-dependent RNA polymerase, which many viruses use to copy their genomes and replicate. It specifically looked for drugs that could bind to a section of the enzyme called the Thumb-1 domain, after identifying that this site is conserved across multiple viruses. “We wanted to find a biological chokepoint – a place where a single drug against a single target could solve dozens of diseases,” says Haders.

By mining data from old papers and patents, the AI named ERA-923 as a potential candidate that could bind to the Thumb-1 domain and thus block viral replication. “Like how OpenAI and Anthropic have downloaded all digital human knowledge, we’ve done the same thing for all of chemistry, biology and clinical pharmacology,” says Haders. He has been applying computational methods to drug design since his PhD research 20 years ago, but says the AI tools available now are “a million times better”.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

To test the AI’s prediction, the researchers measured the activity of the drug, renamed MDL-001, against a range of viruses in infected cells in a lab. They found that it inhibited the influenza A and B viruses, a number of coronaviruses that cause common colds or covid-19, RSV (which causes flu-like symptoms and can be severe in babies), norovirus (also known as the winter vomiting bug) and hepatitis B, C and D, which damage the liver.

MDL-001 also helped treat covid-19 in mice, lowering levels of the underlying virus in their lungs and reducing their weight loss from the illness. It had similar efficacy in mice with hepatitis B and C. Haders will present the results at the Congress of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases in Munich, Germany, in mid-April.

Peter White at the University of New South Wales in Australia is sceptical, since other drugs that have been developed to target the Thumb-1 domain only inhibit hepatitis C, not other viruses. But Model Medicines claims that MDL-001 has a different docking mode that allows it to work across multiple viruses. Daniel Rawle at QIMR Berghofer Medical Research Institute in Brisbane, Australia, also notes that “most antivirals that work in vitro fail in vivo”.

Model Medicines is now planning a clinical trial of MDL-001, which is scheduled to begin early next year. The first step will be to make sure it is safe, although past trials in people with breast cancer have found that it has minimal side effects.

At the moment, viral illnesses are a major burden on well-being and productivity, because people often have to take time off work when they or their children become sick with them. It would be a game changer if these conditions could be rapidly treated with a short course of a multipurpose antiviral pill at home, says Haders. MDL-001 may also be useful in the case of future pandemics of novel coronaviruses or influenza viruses, he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in antiviral treatment, where an AI-discovered repurposed drug shows promise in treating multiple common and serious viral infections, potentially benefiting broad public health and future pandemic preparedness. It provides detailed context on the discovery, mechanism, testing, and planned clinical trials, indicating substantial real-world impact and hope for improved viral illness management.
articles.personality_title: AI rediscovers pill that may fight many common viruses at once
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long searched for medicines that can treat many viruses with one pill. Viruses like the flu, common colds, and stomach bugs all cause illness, but usually need different treatments. A key to stopping many viruses is blocking an enzyme they use to copy themselves.

**What happened** – A company in California used artificial intelligence (AI) to find an old breast cancer drug that might stop many viruses. The drug, now called MDL-001, was tested in labs and in mice. It showed it could block viruses like flu, several coronaviruses (including the one causing COVID-19), RSV (a virus that can make babies very sick), norovirus (which causes stomach problems), and hepatitis viruses that harm the liver. The drug worked by targeting a special part of the virus enzyme shared by many viruses. A clinical trial in people is planned for next year to see if it is safe and effective.

**Impact** – This is the first time one drug has shown it might work against so many different viruses. If successful, it could help people take one pill at home to feel better faster from common viral illnesses, without needing to know exactly which virus they have. This could reduce the time people miss school or work and help during future virus outbreaks.

**What's next step** – The next step is a clinical trial to test MDL-001 in humans, starting early next year. Researchers will check if the drug is safe and if it helps people recover from viral infections. If the trial goes well, the pill might become a new tool for doctors and patients to fight many viruses with one medicine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI has helped find a reused drug that may soon allow one pill to treat many common and serious viral infections safely at home.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une pilule antivirale redécouverte par l’IA pourrait combattre plusieurs virus courants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps un médicament capable de traiter plusieurs virus avec une seule pilule. La grippe, les rhumes et les virus intestinaux provoquent tous des maladies, mais nécessitent souvent des traitements différents. Pour arrêter de nombreux virus, il faut bloquer une enzyme qu’ils utilisent pour se reproduire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise californienne a utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour retrouver un ancien médicament contre le cancer du sein qui pourrait bloquer plusieurs virus. Ce médicament, appelé maintenant MDL-001, a été testé en laboratoire et sur des souris. Il a montré qu’il pouvait bloquer des virus comme la grippe, plusieurs coronavirus (dont celui du COVID-19), le RSV (un virus dangereux pour les bébés), le norovirus (qui cause des troubles digestifs) et les virus de l’hépatite qui endommagent le foie. Il agit en ciblant une partie spéciale d’une enzyme commune à ces virus. Un essai clinique sur des humains est prévu pour l’année prochaine afin de vérifier sa sécurité et son efficacité.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un médicament montre qu’il pourrait agir contre autant de virus différents. S’il réussit, il pourrait permettre aux gens de prendre une seule pilule à la maison pour se sentir mieux plus vite, sans savoir exactement quel virus les a infectés. Cela pourrait réduire le temps perdu à l’école ou au travail et aider en cas de futures épidémies.

**Prochaine étape** – La prochaine étape est un essai clinique sur des humains, qui commencera début de l’année prochaine. Les chercheurs vérifieront si le médicament est sûr et s’il aide les patients à guérir des infections virales. Si l’essai est concluant, la pilule pourrait devenir un nouvel outil pour soigner plusieurs virus avec un seul médicament.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’IA a permis de retrouver un médicament réutilisé qui pourrait bientôt offrir une pilule unique pour traiter plusieurs infections virales courantes et graves en toute sécurité à domicile.
articles.personality_title_es: Una píldora antiviral redescubierta por IA podría combatir varios virus comunes a la vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han buscado durante mucho tiempo un medicamento que pueda tratar muchos virus con una sola píldora. Virus como la gripe, los resfriados comunes y los virus estomacales causan enfermedad, pero normalmente necesitan tratamientos diferentes. Para detener muchos virus, hay que bloquear una enzima que usan para copiarse.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa en California usó inteligencia artificial (IA) para encontrar un viejo medicamento contra el cáncer de mama que podría detener muchos virus. El medicamento, ahora llamado MDL-001, fue probado en laboratorios y en ratones. Mostró que podía bloquear virus como la gripe, varios coronavirus (incluido el que causa COVID-19), RSV (un virus que puede enfermar mucho a los bebés), norovirus (que causa problemas estomacales) y virus de hepatitis que dañan el hígado. El medicamento actúa al atacar una parte especial de la enzima del virus que muchos virus comparten. Se planea un ensayo clínico en personas para el próximo año para ver si es seguro y funciona.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que un medicamento muestra que podría funcionar contra tantos virus diferentes. Si tiene éxito, podría ayudar a la gente a tomar una sola píldora en casa para sentirse mejor más rápido sin saber exactamente qué virus tienen. Esto podría reducir el tiempo que las personas faltan a la escuela o al trabajo y ayudar en futuros brotes de virus.

**Próximo paso** – El siguiente paso es un ensayo clínico para probar MDL-001 en humanos, que comenzará a principios del próximo año. Los investigadores verificarán si el medicamento es seguro y si ayuda a las personas a recuperarse de infecciones virales. Si el ensayo va bien, la píldora podría convertirse en una nueva herramienta para que médicos y pacientes combatan muchos virus con un solo medicamento.

**Resumen en una frase** – La IA ayudó a encontrar un medicamento reutilizado que pronto podría permitir tratar muchas infecciones virales comunes y graves con una sola píldora en casa de forma segura.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing, open pill capsule releasing a gentle stream of interconnected viral RNA strands being gently blocked and tangled by a delicate, intricate molecular lattice shaped like a protective net, set against a warm, neutral background with subtle hints of cells and microscopic virus shapes fading peacefully away.

articles.title: A gene mutation may trap the brain in the wrong reality in schizophrenia patients
articles.summary: A newly identified gene mutation may help explain why schizophrenia patients struggle to update their understanding of reality. The mutation disrupts a brain circuit involved in flexible decision-making, causing mice to stick with outdated choices even when conditions change. Researchers pinpointed the issue to a key thalamus–prefrontal cortex pathway. By reactivating this circuit, they were able to restore normal behavior—raising hope for future therapies.
articles.content:
A common feature of schizophrenia is difficulty using new information to understand the world. This challenge can make decision-making harder and, over time, may contribute to a disconnect from reality.

Researchers at MIT have identified a gene mutation that may play a key role in this problem. In experiments with mice, they found that the mutation disrupts a brain circuit responsible for updating beliefs when new information is received.

The mutation occurs in a gene called grin2a, which had previously been flagged in large genetic studies of schizophrenia. The new findings suggest that targeting this brain circuit could help improve cognitive symptoms associated with the disorder.

"If this circuit doesn't work well, you cannot quickly integrate information," says Guoping Feng, the James W. and Patricia T. Poitras Professor in Brain and Cognitive Sciences at MIT, a member of the Broad Institute of Harvard and MIT, and the associate director of the McGovern Institute for Brain Research at MIT. "We are quite confident this circuit is one of the mechanisms that contributes to the cognitive impairment that is a major part of the pathology of schizophrenia."

Feng and Michael Halassa, an associate professor of psychiatry and neuroscience at Tufts University, are the senior authors of the study, which appears in Nature Neuroscience. Tingting Zhou, a research scientist at the McGovern Institute, and Yi-Yun Ho, a former MIT postdoc, are the lead authors.

Genetic Clues and Schizophrenia Risk

Schizophrenia has a strong genetic component. In the general population, about 1 percent of people develop the condition. That risk increases to 10 percent if a parent or sibling is affected, and rises to 50 percent for identical twins.

Scientists at the Stanley Center for Psychiatric Research at the Broad Institute have identified more than 100 gene variants associated with schizophrenia through genome-wide association studies. However, many of these variants are located in non-coding regions of DNA, making their effects difficult to interpret.

To address this, researchers used whole-exome sequencing, a method that focuses on protein-coding regions of the genome. This approach allowed them to identify mutations directly within genes.

By analyzing around 25,000 sequences from people with schizophrenia and 100,000 from control subjects, the team identified 10 genes where mutations significantly increase the risk of developing the disorder.

How a Gene Mutation Alters Brain Function

In the new study, researchers created mice carrying a mutation in one of those genes, grin2a. This gene produces part of the NMDA receptor, which is activated by the neurotransmitter glutamate and is commonly found on neurons.

Zhou then examined whether these mice showed behaviors similar to those seen in schizophrenia. While symptoms such as hallucinations and delusions (loss of contact with reality) cannot be directly modeled in mice, scientists can study related issues like difficulty interpreting new sensory information.

For years, researchers have proposed that psychosis may result from a reduced ability to update beliefs when new information becomes available.

"Our brain can form a prior belief of reality, and when sensory input comes into the brain, a neurotypical brain can use this new input to update the prior belief. This allows us to generate a new belief that's close to what the reality is," Zhou says. "What happens in schizophrenia patients is that they weigh too heavily on the prior belief. They don't use as much current input to update what they believed before, so the new belief is detached from reality."

Experiment Reveals Slower Decision-Making

To test this idea, Zhou designed a task where mice had to choose between two levers to receive a reward. One lever was low-reward -- mice needed six presses to get one drop of milk. The other offered a higher reward, delivering three drops per press.

At first, all mice preferred the high-reward option. Over time, however, the effort required for that option gradually increased, while the low-reward lever remained unchanged.

Healthy mice adjusted their behavior as conditions shifted. When the effort required for the high-reward option became comparable to the low-reward option, they eventually switched and stayed with the easier choice.

Mice with the grin2a mutation behaved differently. They continued switching back and forth between the options for longer and delayed committing to the more efficient choice.

"We find that neurotypical animals make adaptive decisions in this changing environment," Zhou says. "They can switch from the high-reward side to the low-reward side around the equal value point, while for the animals with the mutation, the switch happens much later. Their adaptive decision-making is much slower compared to the wild-type animals."

Key Brain Circuit Identified

Using functional ultrasound imaging and electrical recordings, the researchers identified the mediodorsal thalamus as the brain region most affected by the mutation. This region connects to the prefrontal cortex, forming a thalamocortical circuit that supports decision-making and executive control.

Neurons in the mediodorsal thalamus appeared to track changes in the value of different choices. The researchers also observed distinct patterns of neural activity depending on whether the mice were exploring options or committing to a decision.

Reversing Symptoms by Activating the Circuit

The team also demonstrated that they could reverse the behavioral effects of the mutation. Using optogenetics, they engineered neurons in the mediodorsal thalamus to respond to light. When these neurons were activated, the mice began to behave more like those without the mutation.

Although only a small fraction of schizophrenia patients carry mutations in grin2a, the researchers suggest that dysfunction in this circuit may represent a shared mechanism underlying cognitive impairments in some patients.

Targeting this pathway could open new possibilities for treatment. The team is now working to identify specific components within the circuit that could be targeted with drugs.

Funding and Future Directions

The research was funded by the National Institutes of Mental Health, the Poitras Center for Psychiatric Disorders Research at MIT, the Yang Tan Collective at MIT, the K. Lisa Yang and Hock E. Tan Center for Molecular Therapeutics at MIT, the Stelling Family Research Fund at MIT, the Stanley Center for Psychiatric Research, and the Brain and Behavior Research Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery identifying a gene mutation that disrupts brain circuits involved in schizophrenia, with experimental evidence showing the possibility to reverse cognitive impairments in mice. This breakthrough has broad implications for understanding and potentially treating schizophrenia, a serious mental health disorder affecting millions worldwide. The study is detailed, focused, and suggests a promising new therapeutic pathway, representing a meaningful positive impact on health.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find gene mutation that affects brain’s decision-making in schizophrenia
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Schizophrenia is a mental health condition that makes it hard for people to understand and respond to changes in the world around them. This difficulty can affect their decisions and connection to reality. Scientists have long searched for the reasons behind these problems.

**What happened** – Researchers at MIT discovered a mutation in a gene called grin2a that disrupts a brain circuit responsible for updating beliefs when new information arrives. They studied mice with this mutation and found these mice struggled to adjust their choices when conditions changed. By activating the affected brain circuit using light, scientists were able to restore normal decision-making in the mice.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows a clear link between a specific gene mutation and the brain’s trouble with flexible thinking in schizophrenia. It points to a part of the brain, the mediodorsal thalamus and its connection to the prefrontal cortex, as key to these problems. This knowledge could help scientists develop new treatments targeting this circuit to improve thinking and decision-making in patients.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to identify exact parts of this brain circuit that drugs could target. Although only some patients have this mutation, similar brain circuit problems may affect others with schizophrenia. Understanding these details could lead to new medicines or therapies to help people better update their understanding of reality.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A gene mutation linked to schizophrenia disrupts a brain circuit for flexible decision-making, but activating this circuit can restore normal behavior in mice, opening paths for new treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une mutation génétique qui affecte la prise de décision dans la schizophrénie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La schizophrénie est une maladie mentale qui rend difficile la compréhension et la réaction aux changements dans le monde. Cela complique la prise de décision et le lien avec la réalité. Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à comprendre les causes de ces difficultés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs du MIT ont découvert une mutation dans un gène appelé grin2a qui perturbe un circuit cérébral essentiel pour mettre à jour les croyances avec les nouvelles informations. Ils ont étudié des souris porteuses de cette mutation et ont constaté que ces souris avaient du mal à ajuster leurs choix quand les conditions changeaient. En activant ce circuit cérébral avec de la lumière, ils ont pu restaurer un comportement normal.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle établit un lien clair entre une mutation génétique spécifique et les difficultés de pensée flexible dans la schizophrénie. Elle montre que le thalamus médiodorsal et son lien avec le cortex préfrontal sont essentiels dans ces problèmes. Ces connaissances pourraient aider à développer de nouveaux traitements ciblant ce circuit pour améliorer la pensée et la prise de décision.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche veut identifier les parties précises de ce circuit cérébral que des médicaments pourraient cibler. Même si seulement certains patients ont cette mutation, des troubles similaires du circuit cérébral pourraient toucher d’autres personnes atteintes de schizophrénie. Comprendre ces détails pourrait mener à de nouveaux médicaments ou thérapies.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une mutation génétique liée à la schizophrénie perturbe un circuit cérébral important pour la prise de décision flexible, mais son activation peut rétablir un comportement normal chez les souris, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren mutación genética que afecta la toma de decisiones en la esquizofrenia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La esquizofrenia es una enfermedad mental que dificulta que las personas comprendan y respondan a los cambios en su entorno. Esto afecta sus decisiones y conexión con la realidad. Los científicos han buscado durante mucho tiempo las causas de estas dificultades.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del MIT encontraron una mutación en un gen llamado grin2a que interfiere con un circuito cerebral que ayuda a actualizar creencias cuando llega nueva información. Estudiaron ratones con esta mutación y vieron que tenían problemas para ajustar sus elecciones cuando las condiciones cambiaban. Al activar este circuito con luz, lograron que los ratones volvieran a tomar decisiones normales.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra una conexión clara entre una mutación genética específica y los problemas del cerebro para pensar de forma flexible en la esquizofrenia. Señala que el tálamo mediodorsal y su conexión con la corteza prefrontal son claves en estos problemas. Esto podría ayudar a crear nuevos tratamientos que mejoren el pensamiento y la toma de decisiones en pacientes.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea identificar partes específicas de este circuito cerebral que puedan ser objetivo de medicamentos. Aunque solo algunos pacientes tienen esta mutación, problemas similares en el circuito cerebral podrían afectar a otros con esquizofrenia. Comprender esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos o terapias.

**Frase clave** – Una mutación genética relacionada con la esquizofrenia interfiere en un circuito cerebral para la toma de decisiones flexible, pero activar este circuito puede restaurar el comportamiento normal en ratones, abriendo caminos para nuevos tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized brain circuit glowing softly, with interconnected glowing pathways symbolizing neural connections between the mediodorsal thalamus and prefrontal cortex, alongside a pair of abstract mice silhouettes navigating a maze of levers and rewards, rendered in gentle natural tones to represent cognitive processes and the gradual adaptation to new information.
